title: SEGMENT 3 — Anatomy of an adenoviral particle: non-enveloped dsDNA in an icosahedral capsid

lines.text: SEGMENT 3 — Anatomy of an adenoviral particle: non-enveloped dsDNA in an icosahedral capsid
Let's open up an adenoviral particle.
Picture, if you can, a soccer ball.
Or more precisely, an icosahedron — a twenty-sided shape with triangular faces.
That's the basic geometry of the adenoviral capsid.
It's about ninety to a hundred nanometers across, which makes it medium-sized as viruses go — about five times bigger than an AAV particle, about a third the size of a herpesvirus particle.
Big enough to see clearly in an electron microscope; small enough that several thousand of them would fit comfortably across the diameter of a human hair.
The capsid is built almost entirely from three proteins.
Hexon is the most abundant — there are 240 copies of it on each virion, tiling the twenty triangular faces.
If you look at an electron micrograph of an adenoviral particle, the surface looks faceted and somewhat textured, and what you're seeing is mostly hexon.
Penton base sits at each of the twelve corners — the vertices of the icosahedron — twelve copies per virion, one per corner.
And sticking out from each penton base, like an antenna or a spike or a tiny stalked mushroom, is the fiber — a long, slender protein with a globular knob at the tip.
That knob is what binds CAR.
Twelve fibers on each virion, one at each vertex.
Hexon, penton, fiber.
That's the visible structure.

Underneath, there are several internal capsid proteins that hold things together — proteins called pIIIa, pVI, pVIII, pIX — and inside the capsid is the genome itself, packaged with two small DNA-binding proteins called pV and pVII that condense and stabilize the DNA.
At the very ends of the genome, attached covalently, is a protein called the terminal protein — TP — that primes DNA replication and stays attached to the genome end like a little cap.
You don't need to memorize the protein names.
What I want you to remember is the architecture: a sturdy outer shell of hexons and pentons and fibers, with a tightly packed DNA core inside.
Now here are two structural features that I want to call out specifically, because they shape everything about adenovirus's behavior as a vector and as an immunogen.
First: the genome inside is double-stranded DNA, about thirty-six thousand base pairs long.
That's 36 kb.
For comparison, AAV's single-stranded DNA genome is about 4.7 kb.
So adenovirus's genome is nearly eight times bigger.
That matters because it determines how much cargo we can fit when we use the virus as a vector.
AAV can carry maybe 4.5 kb of payload before it physically can't be packaged.

Adenovirus — depending on how much of its own genome you delete to make room — can carry anywhere from a few kilobases up to about 36 kb of total foreign DNA in the gutless form we'll discuss in Segment 8.
That capacity advantage is enormous.
It is the main structural reason you might pick adenovirus over AAV for a given job.
Second: adenovirus is non-enveloped.
This is critical.
Many viruses you know — flu, HIV, coronavirus, herpes — wrap themselves in a membrane stolen from the host cell as they bud out.
That membrane envelope is studded with viral surface proteins, but underneath it is essentially a host-derived lipid bilayer.
Enveloped viruses have a soft outer surface and they look, in some sense, partly like a piece of the body.
Adenovirus has no envelope.
It's just protein — a bare, hard, protein shell.
There is no membrane, no stolen lipid layer, no host-derived camouflage.
The capsid is what the immune system sees, undisguised.
The analogy I like: a non-enveloped virus is an armored package that doesn't carry a membrane sticker on the outside.
An enveloped virus is a package wrapped in plain brown paper from the host's own mailroom — it has some host-derived material on the surface that gives it a little camouflage.
Adenovirus arrives in raw armor plating.
No paper.
No sticker.
Just hard, foreign, exposed protein.
Pattern-recognition receptors in your innate immune system are tuned to notice exactly this — they see bare viral protein and they sound the alarm immediately.
That structural fact — non-enveloped, exposed protein capsid — is the deepest reason adenovirus is such a powerful immunogen.
The hexons and pentons on the surface are loud signals.
Antibodies bind them with high affinity.
Complement proteins decorate them.
Macrophages eat them.
And inside the cell, the viral DNA itself — unmethylated bacterial-style DNA — triggers another whole branch of innate immunity through sensors like cGAS-STING.
The body sees adenovirus from multiple angles and responds vigorously.
One more architectural detail before we move on.
The 36-kb genome of adenovirus is divided, organizationally, into a set of regions named for when their genes get expressed during the viral life cycle.
There are early regions — E1, E2, E3, E4 — which are transcribed shortly after the virus enters the cell and which encode regulatory proteins and the viral DNA polymerase.
And there are late regions — L1 through L5 — which kick on later and encode the structural proteins, the hexons and pentons and fibers I just described.
The crucial early region, the one we're going to delete to make first-generation vectors, is E1.
E1 is the master switch.
It encodes proteins, E1A and E1B, that activate transcription of all the other viral genes and that push the host cell into a state hospitable to viral replication.
Delete E1, and the virus can't turn itself on.
It can still infect cells, it can still get its genome into the nucleus, it just can't replicate.
Which is exactly what you want from a vector.
There's also E3, which encodes proteins that help the wild virus evade the host immune system — they down-regulate MHC class I on infected cells, for instance, so cytotoxic T cells can't see them.
E3 is dispensable for replication in tissue culture, so you can delete it freely to make room for cargo.
Deleting E1 and E3 is the original recipe for a first-generation adenoviral vector, and it gives you about eight kilobases of cargo capacity — which is, again, way more than AAV.
So now you have the parts.
Icosahedral capsid, three main capsid proteins, non-enveloped, double-stranded DNA genome of 36 kb, organized into early and late regions, with E1 as the master switch we're going to remove.
Now let's talk about why the immune system pays so much attention to all this protein hardware.
